sets.passage: Ecologists once assumed that a stable ecosystem was simply one that stayed the same year after year. That view has now been largely (1)____. What matters is not whether a system remains unchanged, but whether it can absorb a shock and still (2)____ its essential functions. This capacity is what biologists mean by resilience. A coral reef battered by a storm may lose much of its structure, yet (3)____ recover if enough species survive to rebuild it. Biodiversity is central to this process. A community containing many species is more likely to include some that can (4)____ when conditions shift, so the loss of one organism need not (5)____ a collapse. Scientists call this the insurance effect, (6)____ different species act as a buffer against uncertainty. (7)____, a system dominated by a single crop or a handful of species offers no such protection; one disease can sweep through it (8)____ resistance. The lesson extends (9)____ natural habitats. Economists have borrowed the concept to describe markets and supply chains, arguing that overly efficient systems may be dangerously (10)____. Striving for maximum output, they note, often (11)____ the redundancy that allows recovery after disruption. (12)____, a degree of apparent waste can be the price of long-term survival. This reasoning has practical (13)____. Conservation policy increasingly aims not merely to count species but to protect the variety that keeps a habitat (14)____. Restoring a damaged wetland, for instance, means reintroducing a (15)____ of plants and animals rather than a single showcase species. (16)____ such diversity, the restored site would remain fragile. None of this (17)____ that every species is equally important; some clearly play larger roles. (18)____, the broader point holds. sets.passage: Few areas of public life reveal the gap between knowledge and action as starkly as climate policy. (10)____, some analysts once predicted that disaster itself would force action, on the assumption that visible catastrophe would (11)____ delay. Experience has been less encouraging. sets.passage: Almost everyone who has tried to break a bad habit knows how stubborn behaviour can be. We resolve to exercise, save money, or eat better, and within weeks the old patterns (1)____. If the cue can be removed or (9)____, the habit loses its trigger. Keeping snacks out of sight, for example, works better than (10)____ on self-control to resist them. sets.passage: Anyone who has shopped late in the day, after a long string of choices, may have noticed a curious thing: the decisions get harder, and the temptation to grab whatever is nearest grows (1)____. sets.passage: When we ask why some people live longer and healthier lives than others, the obvious answers, genes and personal habits, tell only part of the story. sets.passage: Human beings are notoriously poor at judging risk, and the errors follow patterns that psychologists have mapped in detail. We do not assess danger by calmly (1)____ probabilities; instead we rely on feelings and mental shortcuts that evolved long before statistics existed. One of the most powerful of these is the availability shortcut, by which we judge an event as likely simply because examples come (2)____ to mind. A single dramatic plane crash, heavily reported, can make air travel feel perilous, (3)____ the figures show it to be remarkably safe. Vivid and recent events thus (4)____ our sense of the odds. People readily accept the considerable dangers of driving or skiing, activities they (6)____ voluntarily, while reacting with alarm to far smaller hazards they feel powerless to (7)____. The presence or absence of control, not the actual numbers, drives much of the (8)____. Dread plays its part too. Threats that are unfamiliar, invisible, or potentially catastrophic provoke a fear out of all (9)____ to their statistical likelihood, which is why a rare new disease can cause more panic than a familiar killer that takes many more lives. These tendencies are not signs of stupidity; they are (10)____ of a mind built for a world of immediate physical dangers rather than abstract numbers. (11)____, they can mislead us badly in a modern society where the gravest risks are often slow and statistical. (12)____, understanding the biases offers a remedy. sets.passage: The popular image of self-control is heroic: a strong will that grits its teeth and (1)____ temptation through sheer effort. Recent psychology paints a more humbling picture. sets.passage: It is tempting to assume that biodiversity matters chiefly for its own sake, a treasure to be admired the way we value a great painting. Economists have therefore tried to put numbers on nature, estimating, for example, what it would (8)____ to replace natural pollination with human labour. sets.passage: For years, the standard advice for living greener was straightforward: buy efficient appliances, recycle diligently, and choose products labelled environmentally friendly. Such steps are not worthless, but a growing number of analysts argue that they (1)____ the deeper problem. The trouble with framing sustainability as a matter of smarter shopping is that it leaves the underlying engine of (2)____ untouched. Modern economies are built to encourage ever more buying, and individual virtue at the checkout can scarcely (3)____ that momentum. Worse, efficiency can sometimes backfire. When a car becomes cheaper to run, people often (4)____ it more, so the savings are partly (5)____ by extra driving, a paradox economists have long noted. Likewise, a household that installs solar panels may feel (6)____ to consume more electricity, reasoning that the energy is now clean. These rebound effects mean that technical gains alone rarely (7)____ total impact as much as expected. (8)____, the conversation is shifting from greener consumption toward consuming (9)____. The most environmentally significant purchase, on this view, is often the one not made at all. Repairing instead of replacing, sharing instead of owning, and simply (10)____ less all reduce demand at its source rather than merely making it cleaner. This argument is uncomfortable (11)____ it questions the assumption that growth and wellbeing must rise together. (12)____, defenders of the conventional approach reply that asking billions of people to consume less is politically (13)____ and may stall the very economies that fund clean technology. There is truth on both sides. Personal restraint, however admirable, cannot (14)____ for the structural changes that only policy and business can deliver. (15)____, those larger changes are unlikely without a shift in what people regard as a (16)____ life. The most honest conclusion is that sustainable consumption requires action at every (17)____ at once: smarter products, fairer rules, and a willingness to (18)____ that more is not always better. Treating any one of these as a complete (19)____ is precisely the kind of comfortable illusion the planet can no longer (20)____.

sets.passage: Scientists often complain that the public misunderstands their work, and they are frequently right. But the old explanation for this gap, that ordinary people are simply ignorant and need to be (1)____ with more facts, has not held up well. Decades of effort to pour information into the public have produced (2)____ results. On many contested issues, supplying people with more data does little to change minds and can even (3)____ existing divisions. The reason, researchers now believe, is that the so-called deficit, a mere shortage of (4)____, is not really the problem. People interpret scientific claims through the (5)____ of their values, identities, and communities. A finding that threatens what someone already (6)____ is likely to be doubted, however solid the evidence. (7)____, accepting it may carry social costs if one's group rejects it. Under these conditions, more information can actually (8)____ disagreement, because better-informed individuals become more skilled at finding reasons to (9)____ what they dislike. This insight has reshaped how experts think about communicating science. The goal, many now argue, is not simply to (10)____ knowledge but to build trust. How a message is delivered, and (11)____ delivers it, can matter as much as its content. A claim is more readily accepted when it comes from a source the audience regards as (12)____ and as sharing their concerns. (13)____, treating the public as an empty vessel to be filled tends to breed resentment rather than understanding. None of this means facts are (14)____; without accurate information no sound judgement is possible. (15)____, facts alone are seldom enough. They must be communicated in a way that respects how people actually (16)____ and decide. A further (17)____ is that scientists themselves must be honest about uncertainty. Overstating confidence may win attention in the short run but (18)____ credibility when claims are later revised. Ultimately, a scientifically literate society is built less on the (19)____ of information than on a relationship of mutual (20)____ between those who produce knowledge and those who must live by it.
